title: SSC CGL T-I English Section Test

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option that rectifies the grammatical error(s) in the given sentence. I wanted to go for a walk, because it was raining heavily.
options: I wanted to go for a walk, as it was raining heavily.
I wanted to go for a walk, unless it was raining heavily.
I wanted to go for a walk, but it was raining heavily.
I wanted to go for a walk, therefore it was raining heavily.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (c). Explanation: The sentence expresses contrast between the desire to go for a walk and the fact that it was raining heavily. Therefore, the conwunction required is but, not because. Correct sentence: I wanted to go for a walk, but it was raining heavily. Grammatical Rule Used (English): Because is used to show reason, whereas but is used to show contrast. When two clauses convey opposite ideas, a contrasting conwunction must be used. Grammatical Rule Used (Hindi): Because कारण बताने के लिए प्रयोग होता है, जबकि but विरोध या विपरीत भाव प्रकट करता है। यहाँ टहलने की इच्छा और बारिश होने के बीच विरोध है, इसलिए but सही है। Explanation of Other Options: (a) as also shows reason, so it does not remove the logical error. (b) unless means “if not”, which does not fit the sentence structure or meaning. (d) therefore shows result, but rain is not the result of wanting to walk. So the correct answer is (c)

questions.question: Select the option that expresses the given sentence in reported speech. Shikha said, “The heavy rain this week has spoiled all my travel plans.”
options: Shikha told me that the heavy rain that week had spoiled all her travel plans.
Shikha said that the heavy rain that week had spoiled all her travel plans.
Shikha said that the heavy rain this week had spoiled all her travel plans.
Shikha said that the heavy rain that week has spoiled all her travel plans.
questions.explanation: The correct indirect speech is (b). Rule Explanation: • “This week” changes to “that week” in indirect speech. • Present Perfect “has spoiled” changes to Past Perfect “had spoiled.” • “My” becomes “her” since Shikha is speaking. Structure: Direct: Subwect + said, “Present Perfect...” Indirect: Subwect + said that + Past Perfect... Example: He said, “The storm has ruined my plan.” → He said that the storm had ruined his plan. �� Info Booster: When reporting from present perfect, we move one step back to past perfect in indirect speech.

questions.question: Select the correct passive form of the given sentence. Issue the advisory to the villagers to be on alert for the cyclone.
options: Let the advisory be issued to the villagers to be on alert for the cyclone.
The advisory will be issued to the villagers to be on alert for the cyclone.
Why hasn’t the advisory been issued to the villagers to be on alert for the cyclone?
The advisory might be issued to the villagers to be on alert for the cyclone.
questions.explanation: The correct passive voice of the imperative sentence is (a). Explanation: The original sentence is an imperative with the verb “Issue” in the base form. To change imperatives to passive voice, we use: Structure: Active Voice (Imperative): Verb + obwect Passive Voice: Let + obwect + be + V3 �� Grammar Booster: ✔️ For imperative voice change, use “Let” at the beginning, followed by the obwect and past participle (V3) of the verb. Example: · Active: Call the doctor. · Passive: Let the doctor be called. Other options are incorrect because: • (b) Future tense – not suitable for imperative passive conversion. • (c) Interrogative structure – not related to original tone. • (d) "might be issued" – speculative; original is a direct command.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate verb to fill in the blank. We regret ______ you that your request for a tax refund has been rewected.
questions.explanation: Explanation The correct option to fill in the blank is (D) to tell. The verb 'regret' followed by a to-infinitive (to tell, to inform, to say) is used to formally announce bad news. (बताने में खेद है) Example: We regret to inform you that the flight has been cancelled. Grammatical Rule Used Regret + Infinitive: Used to express sorrow for something one is about to do (usually giving bad news). Regret + Gerund (-ing): Used to express sorrow about something that happened in the past (e.g., I regret quitting my wob). Meanings of other options • to put: To move something to a specific place; not contextually relevant. (रखना) • to give: To provide someone with something; does not fit the phrase for announcing news. (देना) • to thank: To express gratitude; contradictory to the feeling of regret. (धन्यवाद देना) So the correct answer is (d)

questions.question: Select the correctly spelled form of the word.
questions.explanation: Option (b) is the correctly spelt word. Correct spelling: Logorrhea — excessive and often incoherent talkativeness or wordiness. (Hindi: अतिबातू / अत्यधिक बोलने की आदत) Example: His logorrhea made it difficult for others to get a word in during meetings.

questions.question: Select the correct meaning of the idiom: “Touch with a barge pole.”
options: To handle something with great enthusiasm
To take control of a difficult situation
To avoid involvement or contact completely
questions.explanation: Option (d) is the correct meaning of the given idiom. Touch with a barge pole: to refuse to be involved in or have any contact with something or someone because it is unpleasant or disreputable. (Hindi: किसी चीज़ से दूर रहना या उससे कोई संबंध न रखना) Example: Investors wouldn’t touch the failing company with a barge pole. Meanings of the other options: · (a) To handle something with great enthusiasm – उत्साहपूर्वक संभालना (Incorrect) · (c) To examine something closely – ध्यानपूर्वक जांचना (Incorrect) · (b) To take control of a difficult situation – कठिन परिस्थिति पर नियंत्रण पाना (Incorrect) Other related idioms: • Keep at arm’s length – to avoid close contact or involvement. • Wash one’s hands of – to stop being involved in something.

questions.question: Choose the option that will replace the error highlighted in bold. The meeting was put up due to the sudden illness of the chairman.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (B). The correct substitution of the highlighted words is (B) put off. Explanation: The sentence indicates that the meeting was postponed because the chairman was ill. The phrasal verb 'put off' means to delay or postpone an event to a later time. 'Put up' usually means to build or to tolerate, which does not fit here. Grammatical rule used: Phrasal verb replacement. 'Put off' is the standard English idiom for postponing an engagement. Example: Never put off until tomorrow what you can do today. Information Booster: 'Called off' (Option A) means to cancel something entirely, whereas 'put off' implies a delay. 'Put out' (Option C) means to extinguish a fire or to annoy someone. 'Put away' (Option D) means to store something in its usual place. So the correct answer is (b)

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank. ______ 540,000-square-foot building now houses ______ enormous collection of artworks and historic archives.
questions.explanation: The correct option to fill in the blank is (d) A; an Correct Sentence: A 540,000-square-foot building now houses an enormous collection of artworks and historic archives. Explanation: · First Blank ("A"): · The building is being introduced for the first time in the sentence. · Hence, the indefinite article "A" is used to refer to one of many possible buildings (non-specific). · ✔ “ A 540,000-square-foot building…” · Second Blank ("an"): · The noun "collection" is preceded by the adwective "enormous" which starts with a vowel sound. · Therefore, we use the article "an". · ✔ “…houses an enormous collection…” Why other options are incorrect: · (a) No word required; the – ❌ Article is needed before both nouns. “No word required” before the building is incorrect. · (b) The; no word required – ❌ “The” suggests a specific building already known to the reader, but no such reference is made. · (c) The; a – ❌ “The” is too specific for the first mention of the building, and “a” is incorrect before a vowel sound (“enormous”). Grammar Booster: Use "a" before words that start with a consonant sound, and "an" before words that start with a vowel sound — regardless of spelling. ✔ an apple ✔ a university (because "university" starts with a “yu” sound)

questions.question: The following sentence has been divided into parts. One of them may contain an error. Select the part that contains the error from the given options. If you don’t find any error, mark ‘No error’ as your answer. The museum urgency needs / a curator for the proper maintenance / and documentation of all the collections.
options: a curator for the proper maintenance
and documentation of all the collections
questions.explanation: Option (b) contains an error. Detailed explanation of error: · The phrase “The museum urgency needs” is grammatically incorrect because ‘urgency’ is a noun, but here we need an adverb to modify the verb ‘needs’. · The correct form should be “The museum urgently needs”, where ‘urgently’ (adverb) shows the manner/degree of the need. · So the correct sentence will be: · “The museum urgently needs a curator for the proper maintenance and documentation of all the collections.” Grammatical rule used: · Adwectives describe or qualify nouns/pronouns. · Adverbs describe or modify verbs, adwectives, or other adverbs. · Since ‘needs’ is a verb, it should be modified by an adverb, not a noun or adwective. · Structure: · Subwect + adverb + main verb + obwect · Example: She quickly finished the work. (‘quickly’ modifies verb ‘finished’.) Information booster (Adwective vs Adverb): · Many students confuse forms like urgent / urgently, quick / quickly, easy / easily. · Adwective: urgent, quick, easy → used before nouns ( urgent call, quick response, easy task). · Adverb: urgently, quickly, easily → used before/after verbs ( respond quickly, work easily, needs urgently). · Always check: What is being modified – noun or verb? · If noun → adwective. · If verb → adverb.

questions.question: Identify the incorrectly spelled word:
questions.explanation: Option (c) is incorrectly spelt. Correct spelling: Pseudonym — a fictitious/assumed name used by an author or person. (Hindi: उपनाम) Example: She published her controversial essays under a pseudonym to protect her identity. Meanings of the other options: • (a) Synecdoche: a figure of speech in which a part represents the whole (or vice versa). (Hindi: अलंकार/शब्द-शैली) • (b) Defenestration: the act of throwing someone/something out of a window. (Hindi: खिड़की से बाहर फेंकना) • (d) Cacophony: a harsh, warring mixture of sounds. (Hindi: कर्णकटु ध्वनि)

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank no. (1).
questions.explanation: The word that can be filled in the blank is ‘purpose’ to make the sentence grammatically and contextually correct. • ‘Purpose’ — a meaningful aim or ethical intention behind actions; a sense of mission (उद्देश्य/मकसद). • As per the context, the passage says students should combine profit-making with ethical purpose, which matches the ESG/SDG idea of business with responsibility. Example: A company with clear purpose often earns long-term trust. Meanings of other given words and why they are incorrect: • Purchase – the act of buying (खरीद) — does not fit the idea of ethics and responsibility. • Posture – physical position or an attitude/stance (मुद्रा/रवैया) — not suitable with “ethical” here. • Pastime – a hobby or leisure activity (मनोरंजन) — completely mismatched in meaning.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank no. (2).
questions.explanation: The word that can be filled in the blank is ‘trends’ to make the sentence grammatically and contextually correct. • ‘Trends’ — general directions of change or development over time (रुझान/प्रवृत्तियाँ). • As per the context, “aligned with industry trends” is the standard and logical phrase showing education stays current with market needs. Example: Good curriculum design responds to changing industry trends. Meanings of other given words and why they are incorrect: • Trenches – long narrow ditches (खाइयाँ) — irrelevant to education/industry alignment. • Tremors – small earthquakes/shaking (कंपन) — unrelated to the passage’s meaning. • Triangles – three-sided shapes (त्रिभुज) — meaningless in this context.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank no. (3).
questions.explanation: The word that can be filled in the blank is ‘upskilling’ to make the sentence grammatically and contextually correct. • ‘Upskilling’ — learning new and advanced skills to stay relevant and employable (कौशल-विकास/नई स्किल सीखना). • As per the context, the passage emphasizes adaptability and future readiness, so continuous upskilling fits perfectly. Example: Regular upskilling helps professionals stay competitive in a changing wob market. Meanings of other given words and why they are incorrect: • Upsetting – making someone unhappy/disturbing (परेशान करना) — opposite meaning. • Upturning – turning something upside down (उलट देना) — not related to career development. • Upholding – supporting/maintaining a rule or principle (बनाए रखना) — not the intended idea of learning new skills.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank no. (4).
questions.explanation: The word that can be filled in the blank is ‘catalysts’ to make the sentence grammatically and contextually correct. • ‘Catalysts’ — agents that speed up or enable positive change and progress (प्रेरक/उत्प्रेरक). • As per the context, universities and B-schools are described as powerful enablers that accelerate career growth, so “essential catalysts” fits best. Example: Mentors can act as catalysts for career transformation. Meanings of other given words and why they are incorrect: • Casualties – people harmed or killed (हानी/हताहत) — negative and irrelevant here. • Captions – titles/text under images (शीर्षक/कैप्शन) — unrelated. • Cautions – warnings (चेतावनी) — does not match the positive role described.

questions.question: Select the correct passive form of the given sentence. The pink locusts are attacking the crops in several districts of Rawasthan.
options: The crops in several districts of Rawasthan have been attacked by the pink locusts.
The pink locusts in several districts of Rawasthan are being attacked by the crops.
The crops in several districts of Rawasthan are being attacked by the pink locusts.
Several districts of Rawasthan are being attacked in the crops by the pink locusts.
questions.explanation: The correct passive voice of the given sentence is (c). Explanation of rules: Active Voice: Subwect + is/are/am + V1 + ing + Obwect Passive Voice: Obwect + is/are/am + being + V3 + by + Subwect Structure: · Active: The pink locusts (subwect) + are attacking (present continuous) + the crops (obwect) · Passive: The crops + are being + attacked + by the pink locusts Example: Active: She is cooking the food. Passive: The food is being cooked by her. Info Booster: · “Being” is used in present continuous passive voice to maintain the tense. · Passive verb must always be in past participle (V3) form. Other options are incorrect because: • (a) Changes tense to present perfect – incorrect. • (b) Reverses subwect-obwect relationship – illogical. • (d) Incorrect structure – awkward and ungrammatical phrasing.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate one-word substitution for: “A person who flatters someone in authority for personal gain; a self-seeking flatterer.”
questions.explanation: The correct one-word for the given definition is (d) Sycophant. Sycophant: a person who praises powerful people in a dishonest way to gain advantage. (Hindi: चाटुकार, खुशामदी) Example: The sycophant applauded the boss’s every mistake as “brilliant.” Meanings of the other options: · (a) Ascetic: one who lives a life of strict self-discipline. (Hindi: तपस्वी, संयमी) · (b) Sybarite: a person devoted to luxury and pleasure. (Hindi: विलासी, भोगवादी) · (c) Parasite: one who lives off others; an exploiter. (Hindi: परजीवी)

questions.question: The following sentences of a paragraph are disarranged. Reorder them to form a cohesive and logically structured passage. A. You open it, reach for one, and within seconds, your hand is back in the packet. The craving is rarely driven by hunger. B. A growing body of research suggests that the brain demands more of these ultra-processed foods (UPFS) much like in the way it may crave for nicotine and other addictive substances. C. The world has long known how cigarettes hiwack the brain. Light one, inhale, and the nicotine hit is instant. Within seconds, the chemical acts on the brain to induce a pleasurable sensation. D. And despite the well-documented health risks, many users struggle to kick the habit. Now replace the cigarette with a packet of chips or cookies. E. Some report euphoria or heightened alertness; others, a sense of calm or satisfaction. Over time, the brain begins to crave this effect, leading to nicotine dependence.
questions.explanation: The correct sequence is C–D–E–A–B. · C introduces the base analogy: how cigarettes hiwack the brain—mechanism first (nicotine → instant pleasurable hit). It’s the natural opener setting the neuro-addiction frame. · D extends the cigarette thread: despite known risks, quitting is hard—then pivots with “Now replace the cigarette with a packet of chips or cookies,” signaling a shift from tobacco to food addiction. · E completes the cigarette mechanism with subwective effects (euphoria/alertness/calm) and the outcome— dependence—rounding off the addiction model that the passage is mapping onto food. (Placed here, it still functions as part of the cigarette analogy the paragraph established before the pivot.) · A applies the analogy to UPFs in practice: the chips/cookies behavior loop (hand back in the packet, craving not hunger-driven). · B generalizes and clinches with evidence: research shows the brain demands more UPFs much like it does nicotine—explicitly tying back to the opening analogy. · Set-up → difficulty → mechanism/subwective effects → real-life example → research-backed generalization. · Clear transition cue: “Now replace the cigarette…” in D bridges from cigarettes to UPFs. · Anaphoric cohesion: B’s “these ultra-processed foods” refers back to A’s chips/cookies example.

questions.question: Select the option that expresses the given sentence in indirect speech. My brother said to me, “Don’t play in the rain.”
options: My brother refused me to not play in the rain.
My brother forbade me to play in the rain.
My brother said to me don’t play in the rain.
My brother assured me not to play in the rain.
questions.explanation: The correct indirect speech is (b). Explanation: · For negative imperative sentences (commands/requests with “Don’t”), we use "forbade + obwect + to + verb". · "Said to" becomes "forbade" when expressing prohibition. · We do not use double negatives like "refused me to not..." ❌ · "Assured" is used for giving confidence, not for prohibitions. Grammatical rule used: ➡️ Direct to Indirect (Negative Command): "Don't + verb" → “Subwect + forbade + obwect + to + base form of verb” Example: Direct: She said to me, “Don’t be late.” Indirect: She forbade me to be late. Info Booster: · Never use “not to” after “forbade” – it already implies prohibition. · Avoid incorrect phrases like “refused me to…”

questions.question: Choose the correct passive voice form. “She wants someone to clean the pantry before guests arrive.”
options: She wants the pantry to cleaned before guests arrive.
She wants the pantry cleaned to be before guests arrive.
She wants the pantry to be cleaned before guests arrive.
The pantry is wanted cleaned by her before guests arrive.
questions.explanation: The correct passive voice of the given sentence is (c). Explain the rules of conversion from active to passive with structure in detail. · When an infinitive clause has an obwect (e.g., to clean the pantry), passivizing the obwect yields to be + V3: to clean → to be cleaned. · Keep the matrix verb ( wants) unchanged when only the embedded infinitival clause is passivized. · Structure: · Active (infinitive): Verb + someone + to + V1 + obwect · Passive (infinitive): Verb + obwect + to be + V3 · Example: · Active: “I expect them to finish the task.” · Passive: “I expect the task to be finished.” · Information booster / exceptions: · With verbs of thinking/expecting/believing, we can also use a raising passive: “They expect the team to win” → “The team is expected to win.”

questions.question: Select the most appropriate antonym of the given word “Obdurate”.
questions.explanation: The correct antonym of the given word is (b) Pliant. Obdurate: stubbornly refusing to change one’s opinion or course of action; hard-hearted. (Hindi: हठी/कठोर-हृदय) Example: The obdurate witness refused to revise his statement despite evidence. Pliant: easily bent or influenced; flexible; yielding. (Hindi: लचीला/सहजता से मानने वाला) Example: A pliant attitude helps in resolving conflicts quickly. Synonyms (of obdurate): stubborn, unyielding, adamant, hardened. Antonyms (of obdurate): pliant, compliant, yielding, amenable. Meanings of all the other given options: · (a) Resolute: admirably determined; firm in purpose. (Hindi: दृढ़ निश्चयी) · (c) Implacable: unable to be appeased or calmed; relentless. (Hindi: अडिग/अप्रशम्य) · (d) Intransigent: unwilling to change views or agree; uncompromising. (Hindi: अडियल/समझौता न करने वाला)

questions.question: Select the most appropriate synonym of the given word. “Fiddly”
questions.explanation: The correct synonym of the given word is (a) Complicated. Fiddly: difficult to do or handle because of many small parts or details. (Hindi: झंझटदार, जटिल) Example: Repairing that tiny clock mechanism was really fiddly. Complicated: consisting of many parts and therefore difficult to understand. (Hindi: जटिल, कठिन) Example: The new tax system is complicated and confusing. Synonyms: intricate, complex, tricky, involved. Antonyms: simple, easy, straightforward, plain. Meanings of all the other given options: • (b) Smooth: having an even surface; not rough. (Hindi: चिकना, मुलायम) • (c) Unimportant: lacking significance or value. (Hindi: महत्वहीन, तुच्छ) • (d) Durable: able to withstand wear or damage. (Hindi: टिकाऊ, मजबूत)

questions.question: The following sentences of a paragraph are disarranged. Reorder them to form a cohesive and logically structured passage. A. There is no denying that it is the era of automation; we have adopted technology in our lives, and the wourney has only been rewarding, so why not the much-needed automation for our bread-and-butter sector, "Textiles"? B. As we head out for a morning walk, our smartwatch tracks our heart rate and calories burned, helping us meet our health goals. C. As we return, another touch and the hot water is all set for a refreshing bath. As we relax and sit back browsing on our phones, global news from all sectors appears. D. As we scroll through multiple notifications, right from bill payments to the important buyer mails, everything is wust a click away.
questions.explanation: The correct sequence is BCDA. · B → Starts the day naturally: “As we head out for a morning walk…” introduces the earliest activity. It also sets the tech theme with the smartwatch tracking health metrics. · C → Follows chronologically after returning from the walk: “As we return, another touch and the hot water is all set…” Then we relax and browse news—still within the morning routine and still highlighting everyday automation. · D → Continues the same relaxed, post-bath phone usage: “As we scroll through multiple notifications…” (bills, buyer mails) shows further tech-enabled conveniences. · A → Concludes with a generalization and persuasive pivot: “There is no denying that it is the era of automation… so why not the much-needed automation for our… ‘Textiles’?” This sentence summarizes the preceding examples and extends the argument to the target sector, making it a logical wrap-up. Why BCDA works: The temporal markers (“As we head out…”, “As we return…”, “As we scroll…”) create a clear morning timeline (walk → return/bath → phone notifications). After establishing multiple everyday instances of automation (B, C, D), sentence A leverages those examples to argue for automation in textiles, serving as the concluding thesis.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate antonym of the given word. “Peremptory”
questions.explanation: The correct antonym of the given word is (c) Hesitant. Peremptory: expecting immediate obedience; abrupt and bossy. (Hindi: आदेशात्मक, रूखा) Example: The officer gave a peremptory order to leave at once. Hesitant: unsure, slow, or unwilling to act quickly. (Hindi: हिचकिचाने वाला, संकोची) Example: She was hesitant to accept the offer without details. Synonyms: abrupt, imperious, authoritarian, curt. Antonyms: hesitant, tentative, unsure, indecisive. Meanings of all the other given options: • (a) Imperious: arrogantly domineering. (Hindi: अहंकारी, हुक्म चलाने वाला) • (b) Dictatorial: behaving like a dictator; tyrannical. (Hindi: तानाशाही) • (d) Commanding: having authority; giving orders. (Hindi: आदेश देने वाला, प्रभावशाली)

questions.question: Select the sentence containing the homonym of the highlighted word: The antiquary purchased a faded scrip issued by a defunct railway company.
options: The pharmacist refused to fill the scrip without a signature.
The collector framed the scrip beside the old tickets.
That scrip once circulated as a substitute for cash.
The scrip bore a serial number and a printed seal.
questions.explanation: The correct option is (a). Meanings of the Word: SCRIP: (1) A substitute for legal tender; a certificate or voucher issued in place of money. (2) (Informal) A prescription. The highlighted word scrip in the given sentence means a voucher/certificate used like money. Another meaning of scrip is a prescription. Option analysis: A uses scrip to mean a prescription, which is different from the voucher meaning, so it correctly uses the homonym. B, C and D use scrip in the voucher/certificate sense, matching the original meaning. Example (homonym usage): He misplaced the scrip and could not collect the medicine.
